truth.papers.A58.title: Duplex microstructure enhanced mechanical property and underlined mechanism in Fe35MnxAl0.1C low-density steel
truth.papers.A58.paper_scope: This study investigates the microstructure and mechanical properties of Fe-35Mn-xAl-0.1C (x=4,6,8,10 wt%) low-density steels, focusing on the duplex microstructure, dislocation type (edge vs. screw), slip band refinement, and strain hardening mechanisms to achieve an excellent combination of strength and ductility.

prediction.papers.A58.title: Duplex microstructure enhanced mechanical property and underlined mechanism in Fe35MnxAl0.1C low-density steel
prediction.papers.A58.paper_scope: Investigation of microstructure, mechanical properties, and deformation mechanisms in Fe-35Mn-xAl-0.1C (x=4,6,8,10) low-density steels, focusing on edge dislocation activation and dynamic slip band refinement in ferrite-austenite duplex 8Al steel.

truth.alloys.alloy_4al.alloys_notes: Nominal composition also includes 0.1Nb, 0.1V, 1Mo, 1W (wt%). Phase compositions determined by EPMA given in Table 1.
truth.alloys.alloy_6al.alloys_notes: Nominal composition also includes 0.1Nb, 0.1V, 1Mo, 1W (wt%). Phase compositions determined by EPMA given in Table 1.

prediction.alloys.alloy_4al.alloys_notes: Measured composition (wt.%): Mn 33.92, Al 4.13, C 0.094, Nb 0.11, V 0.11, Mo 1.20, W 0.98, Fe Bal. Investigated phase composition by EPMA not listed separately for γ/α because single-phase austenite.
prediction.alloys.alloy_6al.alloys_notes: Measured bulk composition (wt.%): Mn 35.14, Al 5.97, C 0.095, Nb 0.11, V 0.10, Mo 1.24, W 0.95, Fe Bal. Phase compositions by EPMA: γ (Mn 35.92, Al 5.88, C 0.098, Nb 0.09, V 0.09, Mo 1.12, W 0.86), α (Mn 32.44, Al 6.83, Nb 0.09, V 0.18, Mo 1.54, W 1.63).
prediction.alloys.alloy_8al.alloys_notes: Measured bulk composition (wt.%): Mn 34.56, Al 8.10, C 0.076, Nb 0.11, V 0.11, Mo 1.31, W 1.27, Fe Bal. Phase compositions by EPMA: γ (Mn 36.06, Al 7.10, C 0.253, Nb 0.08, V 0.10, Mo 0.84, W 0.89), α (Mn 33.84, Al 8.26, Nb 0.08, V 0.11, Mo 1.25, W 1.25).
prediction.alloys.alloy_10al.alloys_notes: Measured bulk composition (wt.%): Mn 34.66, Al 10.17, C 0.097, Nb 0.11, V 0.11, Mo 1.30, W 1.38, Fe Bal. Phase compositions by EPMA: γ (Mn 37.39, Al 8.51, Nb 0.08, V 0.09, Mo 0.80, W 0.81), α (Mn 34.13, Al 10.18, Nb 0.08, V 0.11, Mo 1.24, W 1.26).

truth.processes.proc_solution_treated.descript: As-cast ingots homogenized at 1150°C for 4 h, hot-forged into rods of 16.5 mm diameter, solution-treated at 1050°C for 2 h, oil quenched.

prediction.processes.proc_solution_treated.descript: Vacuum induction melting under high-purity Ar; homogenization at 1150°C for 4 h; hot-forging into rods of 16.5 mm diameter; solution treatment at 1050°C for 2 h followed by oil quenching.
prediction.processes.proc_6al_01.description: Vacuum induction melting under high-purity Ar; homogenization at 1150°C for 4 h; hot-forging into rods of 16.5 mm diameter; solution treatment at 1050°C for 2 h followed by oil quenching.
prediction.processes.proc_8al_01.description: Vacuum induction melting under high-purity Ar; homogenization at 1150°C for 4 h; hot-forging into rods of 16.5 mm diameter; solution treatment at 1050°C for 2 h followed by oil quenching.
prediction.processes.proc_10al_01.description: Vacuum induction melting under high-purity Ar; homogenization at 1150°C for 4 h; hot-forging into rods of 16.5 mm diameter; solution treatment at 1050°C for 2 h followed by oil quenching.

truth.processing_steps.sample_4al.processing_s: Forged into rods of 16.5 mm diameter.

truth.structures.sample_4al.overall_structure: Single-phase face-centered cubic (FCC) austenite with quasi-equiaxed grains containing annealing twin boundaries.
truth.structures.sample_4al.microstructure_lis: quasi-equiaxed grains with annealing twin boundaries
In austenite matrix, indicated by satellite diffraction spots near γ diffraction spots; spinodal decomposition of Al and C
Dislocation cell blocks form after tensile deformation, typical of low-carbon austenitic lightweight steel.
truth.structures.sample_6al.overall_structure: Austenite-based duplex lightweight steel: predominantly austenite with some ferrite; austenite contains annealing twins.
truth.structures.sample_6al.microstructure_lis: quasi-equiaxed grains with annealing twin boundaries
In austenite, indicated by weak satellite diffraction spots at {110} positions
DO3 ordered domains (coexisted with B2 domains)
In ferrite matrix, maximum size ~2.0 nm; both B2 and DO3 superlattice reflections present
dislocation cell blocks and planar slip
Austenite: dislocation cell blocks. Ferrite: dislocations exhibit planar slip characteristics, but less pronounced than in 8Al.
truth.structures.sample_8al.overall_structure: Deformed microstructure after tensile straining (3% to 28% engineering strain). Ferrite: planar slip bands composed of edge, mixed, and screw dislocations; dynamic slip band refinement; dislocation pairing due to shearing of DO3 domains. Austenite: dislocation cell blocks, dislocation cells, and a small amount of deformation twins at high strain.
truth.structures.sample_8al.microstructure_lis: In austenite, satellite diffraction spots near γ spots; intensity decreases with increasing nominal Al content
DO3 ordered domains (coexisted with B2 domains)
In ferrite matrix, maximum size ~2.1 nm; DO3 domains coexist within both B2 and ferrite matrix; shearable by dislocations

Ferrite: edge, mixed, and screw dislocations exhibit planar slip; leading dislocations in slip bands paired (up to 3 pairs); slip band spacing refines from ~780 nm (0% strain) to ~80 nm (28% strain); cross-slip activated at high strains. Austenite: dislocation cell blocks refine from ~900 nm to ~240 nm; dislocation cells refine from ~280 nm to ~220 nm; few deformation twins formed near maximum force.
truth.structures.sample_10al.overall_structure: Nearly fully ferrite (98.1% ferrite by XRD) with small amount of austenite; contains acicular 18R martensite precipitates and coarse DO3 ordered domains.
truth.structures.sample_10al.microstructure_li: DO3 ordered domains (coexisted with B2 domains)
In ferrite matrix, large DO3 domains up to ~19 nm; cannot be completely sheared by dislocations
Acicular precipitates within ferrite grains; enriched in Mn and C, depleted in Al; formed during quenching
In austenite, similar to other steels

Ferrite: predominantly long straight screw dislocations with localized non-uniform distribution; pronounced dislocation pairing (up to 6 pairs); planar slip but brittle fracture. Austenite: complex planar slip characteristics during early deformation.

prediction.structures.sample_4al.overall_structure: Single-phase austenite (FCC) lightweight steel with quasi-equiaxed grains and annealing twins.
prediction.structures.sample_4al.microstructure_lis: L1_2 short-range ordered domains; spinodal decomposition of Al and C
prediction.structures.sample_6al.overall_structure: Austenite-based duplex lightweight steel with FCC austenite matrix and BCC ferrite containing ordered domains.
prediction.structures.sample_8al.overall_structure: Ferrite-based duplex lightweight steel with ~75.7 mass% BCC ferrite and ~24.3 mass% FCC austenite; ferrite contains B2 and DO3 ordered domains.
prediction.structures.sample_10al.overall_structure: Nearly fully ferrite lightweight steel with ~98.1 mass% BCC ferrite; ferrite contains B2 and DO3 ordered domains and acicular 18R martensite precipitates.

truth.interfaces.sample_8al.defect_interaction: Edge dislocations activation at ferrite-austenite grain boundaries

High back stresses in soft austenite and forward stresses in hard ferrite generate high stresses at boundaries; stress multiplication facilitates collective glide of dislocations on the same slip plane, promoting planar slip and edge dislocation multiplication.
truth.interfaces.sample_8al.interface_notes[0]: Heterogeneous deformation generates high stress at ferrite grain boundaries; austenite (24.3%) acts as soft phase, effectively alleviating stress localization in ferrite and enabling deformation to higher stresses.
truth.interfaces.sample_8al.interface_notes[1]: Edge dislocations more readily activated at grain boundaries, facilitating slip band propagation and homogeneous deformation.

prediction.interfaces.sample_6al.defect_interaction: Austenite-based duplex structure with deformation compatibility between ferrite and austenite phases.
prediction.interfaces.sample_8al.defect_interaction: edge dislocation activation at ferrite-austenite phase boundary

High stresses at ferrite grain boundaries and stress-multiplication effect facilitate collective glide of dislocations on the same slip plane; soft austenite phase (~24.3%) alleviates stress localization in hard ferrite.
prediction.interfaces.sample_8al.interface_notes[0]: Back stresses generated in soft austenite and forward stresses in hard ferrite during deformation of heterogeneous structure.

truth.properties.sample_4al.mechanical.tensile: longitudinal (tensile axis parallel to rod axis)

prediction.properties.sample_4al.mechanical.tensile: Engineering stress-strain curve and true stress-strain curve shown in Fig. 3.
Typical three-stage strain hardening (stages A, D, E)
prediction.properties.sample_6al.mechanical.tensile: Engineering stress-strain curve and true stress-strain curve shown in Fig. 3.
Typical three-stage strain hardening (stages A, D, E)
prediction.properties.sample_8al.mechanical.tensile: Engineering stress-strain curve and true stress-strain curve shown in Fig. 3.
Five-stage strain hardening (stages A, B, C, D, E); stage C shows increased hardening due to dynamic slip band refinement in ferrite
prediction.properties.sample_10al.mechanical.tensil: Engineering stress-strain curve and true stress-strain curve shown in Fig. 3.
Rapidly decreasing strain hardening rate leading to brittle failure

prediction.performance.sample_8al.comparative_perfo: Compared to 10Al, 8Al achieves superior strength-ductility balance: UTS 740 MPa vs 613 MPa and total elongation 39.8% vs 4.6%, despite lower yield strength (455 MPa vs 585 MPa).

truth.characterization_methods.idx_0.technique: EBSD in SEM (JEOL JSM-7800F) with step size 1 μm; data analyzed by HKL Channel 5 software
truth.characterization_methods.idx_0.purpose: Phase distribution and grain morphology analysis for all steels
truth.characterization_methods.idx_0.key_findi: 4Al: single FCC austenite; 6Al: austenite-based duplex; 8Al: ferrite-based duplex (75.7% ferrite); 10Al: nearly fully ferrite (98.1% ferrite). Quasi-equiaxed grains with annealing twin boundaries in austenite.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_1.technique: TEM (FEI Tecnai G2 F20 and FEI Talos F200X) operated at 200 kV, including selected area diffraction, dark-field imaging, and two-beam diffraction analysis
truth.characterization_methods.idx_1.purpose: Characterization of phases, ordered domains, dislocation types and deformation substructures
truth.characterization_methods.idx_1.key_findi: L12 short-range order in austenite of all steels; B2 and DO3 ordered domains in ferrite; DO3 domain sizes: 2.0 nm (6Al), 2.1 nm (8Al), 19 nm (10Al). 10Al contains 18R martensite. Dislocation analysis: 8Al ferrite activated edge, mixed and screw dislocations forming slip bands; 10Al ferrite mainly long straight screw dislocations. Planar slip characteristics observed in ferrite. Dislocation pairing occurs when shearing DO3 domains.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_2.purpose: Phase identification and mass fraction quantification
truth.characterization_methods.idx_2.key_findi: 8Al steel: ferrite mass fraction 75.7%, austenite 24.3%. 10Al steel: ferrite mass fraction 98.1%.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_3.technique: Tensile testing using MTS machine at room temperature, strain rate 1.0×10^{-3} s^{-1}, standard specimens with 5 mm diameter and 25 mm gauge length; tensile axis parallel to rod axis; at least three tests per condition
truth.characterization_methods.idx_3.purpose: Determination of mechanical properties (yield strength, tensile strength, elongation) and strain hardening behavior
truth.characterization_methods.idx_3.key_findi: 4Al: YS 239 MPa, UTS 552 MPa, uniform elongation 38.5%. 6Al: YS 251 MPa, UTS 589 MPa, uniform elongation 37.6%. 8Al: YS 455 MPa, UTS 740 MPa, uniform elongation 31.0%. 10Al: YS 585 MPa, UTS 613 MPa, uniform elongation 2.1%. 8Al exhibits five-stage strain hardening, 4Al and 6Al three-stage.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_4.purpose: Measure microhardness of austenite and ferrite phases
truth.characterization_methods.idx_4.key_findi: Microhardness of ferrite significantly higher than austenite; values: 4Al γ 150 HV; 6Al γ 155 HV, α 248 HV; 8Al γ 185 HV, α 268 HV; 10Al γ 210 HV, α 330 HV. Ferrite hardness increases with Al content.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_5.purpose: Determine compositions of austenite and ferrite phases
truth.characterization_methods.idx_5.key_findi: Phase-specific compositions given in Table 1 (6Alγ, 6Alα, 8Alγ, 8Alα, 10Alγ, 10Alα). Austenite enriched in Mn and C, ferrite enriched in Al.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_6.key_findi: Density decreases with increasing Al: 4Al 7.42 g/cm^3, 6Al 7.18 g/cm^3, 8Al 6.99 g/cm^3, 10Al 6.84 g/cm^3.

prediction.characterization_methods.sample_6al.key_: Austenite hardness 155 HV; ferrite hardness 248 HV.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_8al.key_: Austenite hardness 185 HV; ferrite hardness 268 HV.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_10al.key: Austenite hardness 210 HV; ferrite hardness 330 HV.

truth.computational_details.idx_0.simulation_m: Thermodynamic calculation of stacking fault energy
truth.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Subregular solution thermodynamic model for SFE

ρ is molar surface density along {111} planes; ΔG^{γ→ε} is molar Gibbs energy of γ→ε transformation; σ is interfacial energy per unit area of γ/ε phase boundary. Thermodynamic functions and parameters from literature [35].
Calculated SFE at 23°C: 4Alγ 51.25 mJ/m², 6Alγ 62.64 mJ/m², 8Alγ 68.47 mJ/m². 10Alγ not available.
truth.computational_details.sample_8al.simulat: Analytical strengthening model combining ferrite slip band refinement and austenite dislocation cell refinement
truth.computational_details.sample_8al.validat: Comparison of calculated and measured true stress-strain curves in Fig. 9(b); good agreement achieved.
truth.computational_details.sample_8al.analyti: σ_0 = 455 MPa (yield stress); mass fractions: ferrite 75.7%, austenite 24.3%.
Deformation-independent term σ_0 equals measured yield strength.
σ_α(ε) = K M G b / D
K = 0.75 (similitude coefficient, fitted); M = 2.8 (Taylor factor); G = 80.7 GPa (shear modulus); b = 0.25 nm (Burgers vector); D is mean slip band spacing (from Fig. 6).
K = 0.75 very close to K = 0.7 reported for Fe30.4Mn8Al1.2C austenitic lightweight steel, confirming dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR) mechanism.
σ_γ(ε) = K_SB M_SB G b / D + 0.8 K_DCB M_DCB G b / D + 0.2 K_DC M_DC G b / D

K_SB+DCB = 2.03, K_DC = 2.02 (determined from 4Al steel); M_SB = M_DCB = 3.06, M_DC = 2.44; G = 70 GPa; b = 0.25 nm; D corresponds to slip band spacing, dislocation cell block size, or dislocation cell size (Fig. 6). Factors 0.8 and 0.2 are area fractions of DCB and DC.
Similarity coefficients K essentially unchanged for high-Mn steels with similar deformation structures; values close to 2.0–2.9 reported for austenitic stainless steels.

prediction.computational_details.idx_0.simulation_m: Thermodynamic calculation of stacking fault energy (γ_SFE)
prediction.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Calculated values at 23°C: 4Al_γ = 51.25 mJ·m⁻², 6Al_γ = 62.64 mJ·m⁻², 8Al_γ = 68.47 mJ·m⁻². 10Al_γ not calculated because austenite fraction is negligible.
prediction.computational_details.sample_8al.simulat: Analytical flow stress model based on slip band and dislocation cell refinement
prediction.computational_details.sample_8al.validat: Best fit validated against measured true stress data in Table 4 and Fig. 9(b).
prediction.computational_details.sample_8al.analyti: Composite flow stress model for 8Al duplex steel
σ_tot = σ_0 + 0.757σ_α(ε) + 0.243σ_γ(ε); σ_(α) = K M G b / D; σ_(γ) = K_SB M_SB G b / D + 0.8 K_DCB M_DCB G b / D + 0.2 K_DC M_DC G b / D
Ferrite: K=0.75, M=2.8, G=80.7 GPa, b=0.25 nm. Austenite: K_SB+DCB=2.03, K_DC=2.02, M_SB=M_DCB=3.06, M_DC=2.44, G=70 GPa, b=0.25 nm. Best fit with σ_0=455 MPa.
Calculated flow stress agrees well with measured data, confirming dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR) mechanism in ferrite.

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: The 8Al steel exhibits a five-stage strain hardening behavior (stages A-E), closely resembling that of high-carbon high-aluminum austenitic lightweight steels, and is attributed to the dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR) mechanism in ferrite.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Slip band spacing in ferrite of 8Al steel refines from 780 nm at 0% engineering strain to 300 nm at 10%, 120 nm at 20%, and 80 nm at 28% strain. Austenite dislocation cell block size refines from 900 nm to 240 nm, and dislocation cell size from 280 nm to 220 nm.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: In 10Al steel, the coarsened DO3 ordered domains (~19 nm) cannot be fully sheared; only a few long straight screw dislocations are activated, leading to brittle fracture. Ductility is also severely degraded by 18R martensite precipitates.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: The number of dislocation pairs (up to 6 in 10Al, up to 3 in 8Al) corresponds to the DO3 domain size; using D = N b with b=0.25 nm gives calculated domain sizes of 1.5 nm for 8Al (close to TEM-observed 2.1 nm) and 3.0 nm for 10Al (much smaller than TEM 19 nm, indicating large domains are incompletely sheared).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: In 4Al steel, the similitude coefficients K_SB+DCB = 2.03 and K_DC = 2.02 derived from the slip band/dislocation cell refinement relation closely match values reported for austenitic stainless steels (2.0–2.9).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Microhardness of austenite in 4Al and 8Al increases monotonically with strain, while ferrite in 8Al initially softens then hardens, indicating differences in deformation mechanisms between the two phases.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: The short-range ordered L12 structure in austenite was detected by satellite diffraction spots near the [100]γ zone axis; its intensity decreases with increasing nominal Al content.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Acicular 18R martensite in 10Al steel is significantly enriched in Mn and C and depleted in Al, as revealed by TEM EDS mapping.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: The stacking fault energy of austenite increases with Al content from 51.25 mJ/m² (4Al) to 68.47 mJ/m² (8Al) at room temperature.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: The densities of investigated steels are 7.42, 7.18, 6.99, and 6.84 g/cm³ for 4Al, 6Al, 8Al, and 10Al, respectively, measured by Archimedes principle.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Densities measured by Archimedes principle: 4Al = 7.42 g·cm⁻³, 6Al = 7.18 g·cm⁻³, 8Al = 6.99 g·cm⁻³, 10Al = 6.84 g·cm⁻³.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Calculated stacking fault energies of austenite at room temperature (Table 2): 4Al_γ = 51.25 mJ·m⁻², 6Al_γ = 62.64 mJ·m⁻², 8Al_γ = 68.47 mJ·m⁻².
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: DO3 ordered domain sizes in ferrite from TEM dark-field images: ~2.0 nm in 6Al, ~2.1 nm in 8Al, and ~19 nm in 10Al; domain size increases with Al content.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: 18R martensite acicular precipitates observed in 10Al ferrite after quenching are significantly enriched in Mn and C and depleted in Al; this martensite is identified as a ductility-reducing phase.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Dislocation pairing in ferrite due to APB energy: up to 3 pairs in 8Al and up to 6 pairs with ~3 nm spacing in 10Al. Estimated ordered domain sizes from pairing: ~1.5 nm (8Al) and ~3.0 nm (10Al), with 8Al value close to TEM observation (~2.1 nm) but 10Al value much smaller than TEM observation (~19 nm), indicating 10Al domains are difficult to completely shear.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Microhardness evolution with true strain (Fig. 3e): 8Al ferrite initially softens then hardens, while austenite hardness increases monotonically; 4Al austenite hardens monotonically.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Strengthening contributions to 8Al flow stress (Table 4): at 3.0% engineering strain, σ_(α)=41 MPa and σ_(γ)=28 MPa; at 28.0% engineering strain (24.7% true strain, true stress 924 MPa), σ_(α)=347 MPa and σ_(γ)=111 MPa.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: Average slip band spacing in ferrite (α_SB), dislocation cell block size (γ_SB+DCB), and dislocation cell size (γ_DC) for 8Al at engineering strains 0%, 10%, 20%, 28% documented in Fig. 6: e.g., α_SB decreases from 780 nm to 80 nm.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: Satellite diffraction spots in austenite SADPs (Fig. 1e-h) indicate spinodal decomposition and L1_2 short-range ordering of Al and C; intensity decreases with increasing nominal Al content.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_10.value: The 10Al steel exhibits brittle fracture caused by activation of only a few long straight screw dislocations, whereas 8Al ferrite shows homogeneous distribution of edge and mixed dislocations that enable extensive plastic deformation and high ductility.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_11.value: Tensile testing used standard specimens with 5 mm diameter and 25 mm gauge length machined from water-quenched rods; tensile axis parallel to rod axis. Interrupted tensile tests conducted to engineering strains of 3%, 10%, 20%, and 28% for 8Al.
